name: Omar
note: SYNTHETIC — draft for clinical advisor review. De-identified/fictional. Deliberately inconsistent: the report should capture the night/anticipatory pattern WITHOUT over-stating daytime fear or inventing a clean story.

entries.child_behavior_observed: Omar was terrified and got into our bed shaking.
entries.parent_response: We let him sleep with us.

entries.child_behavior_observed: Omar watched the lightning from the window and seemed fine, even said it was cool.

entries.situation: Power went out in the evening
entries.child_behavior_observed: Omar screamed in the dark, said monsters were there, and wouldn't move until we got a flashlight.
entries.parent_response: We got flashlights and stayed with him.

entries.child_behavior_observed: Some nights he's fine; other nights he says he can't and needs the hall light on and the door open. It's inconsistent.
entries.parent_response: We leave the hall light on most nights now.

entries.child_behavior_observed: Omar kept asking if it would storm and wanted to sleep in our room 'just in case,' even though it was sunny out.
entries.parent_response: We said he could if he was scared.

situations: Darkness / power outages / sleeping with lights off
parental_responses: Co-sleeping / letting him into the parents' bed
Providing lights / flashlights / leaving doors open
expected_elements.notes: DRAFT answer key — advisor to confirm/correct. KEY GRADING POINT: daytime storms and some nights are low-distress; a good report should reflect the night/anticipatory pattern and the inconsistency, and must NOT fabricate a uniformly severe storm phobia.
